feedback.text: Hello. I am writing to share a concern regarding the proposed Digital Fairness Act, particularly in relation to its potential impact on digital items and marketplaces that are not connected to gambling mechanics. While I fully support the goal of protecting minors from exploitative digital practices such as loot boxes and similar chance-based systems, I believe there is a risk thatif not carefully wordedthe legislation could unintentionally affect digital assets that function very differently. A growing portion of the gaming community, such as myself, treats certain digital items (such as cosmetic skins or collectibles) as investment assets, much like cryptocurrencies. These items are often acquired and traded without the involvement of gambling mechanics, and they hold real-world value through open peer-to-peer marketplaces. In fact, many of these platforms operate fully within existing legal frameworks and provide transparency and safeguards like identity verification. If the Acts definitions are too broad, it could restrict or even penalize legitimate markets and users who have invested significant funds into digital items with long-term value. This could lead to economic harm for consumers and businesses alike, especially in cases where digital assets are held as part of personal portfolios or entrepreneurial activity. Therefore I would respectfully ask the Commission to clearly distinguish between chance-based mechanics (e.g., loot boxes) and open-market tradable items. Thank you for your attention to this matter. I hope the final version of the Act will reflect both the need to protect vulnerable users and the importance of preserving legitimate digital ownership. Sincerely, An EU citizen
